name: Partitive Articles (some/any)
summary: Use du, de la, and des to talk about some or an unspecified amount of something.
explanation:
When you talk about an unspecified quantity, 'some' or 'any', French uses the partitive article: du (masculine), de la (feminine), de l' (before a vowel), and des (plural). Je mange du pain (I eat some bread), je bois de l'eau (I drink some water).

English often leaves 'some' out, but French does not: you must say je veux du café, not je veux café.

After a negative, the partitive becomes simply de (or d' before a vowel): je ne mange pas de pain (I don't eat bread). This change to de also happens after expressions of quantity: beaucoup de, un peu de.
pattern: du (m) / de la (f) / de l' (vowel) / des (pl)  |  negative: de / d'

examples.en: You are eating some pineapple. (before a vowel)

examples.native: Je ne bois pas de café.
examples.en: I don't drink coffee. (de after a negative)

examples.native: Il y a beaucoup de monde.
examples.en: There are a lot of people. (de after a quantity)

commonMistakes: Dropping 'some' as in English: it is je veux du café, not je veux café.
Keeping du or de la after a negative: it is pas de pain, not pas du pain.
Using des after an expression of quantity: it is beaucoup de pommes, not beaucoup des pommes.
Confusing the partitive du with the contraction du (de + le, 'of the').
Forgetting de l' before a noun starting with a vowel.
tip: If you could insert 'some' or 'any' in English, French needs a partitive. After a negative, it all collapses to de.
